topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights, usually a warm invitation, felt like harsh interrogation lamps. I stood before the canvas, my jaw tight enough to crack bone. It was *my* piece, the culmination of six months of sleepless nights and agonizing creation, now hanging there, bathed in the indifferent glow of strangers' appreciation. A man in a tailored suit was leaning in, nodding thoughtfully, his expression one of detached admiration. I clenched my fists so hard my nails bit into my palms, the fine tremor running up my arms. "Excuse me," I managed, my voice a strained whisper that barely carried over the hushed murmurs. "This is mine. You have no right to display it." His smile didn't falter; it was infuriatingly placid. I took a sharp breath, tasting the metallic tang of rising anger, and turned away, the silence of my retreat deafening.

[story 2]

I walked into the space and the air immediately thickened with a suffocating pressure. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that mirrored the churning in my stomach. There, dominating the wall, was my vision, carelessly positioned next to some aggressively modern sculpture I didn't recognize. A woman pointed, her gesture dismissive, as if my entire existence was merely an object to be cataloged. A heat began to bloom behind my eyes, a blinding, hot sensation that made the edges of the room swim. "How dare you?" I hissed, stepping forward, my boots clicking sharply on the polished floor. My knuckles were white where I gripped the strap of my worn portfolio. I wanted to scream, to dismantle the display, to make them feel the sheer weight of my violation, but I kept my voice dangerously low, every syllable laced with a brittle, controlled intensity.

[story 3]

The velvet rope felt flimsy beneath my grip as I surveyed the scene. My artwork, vibrant and raw, was positioned awkwardly, overshadowed by larger, more commercial pieces. A collector was circling it slowly, murmuring about technique, completely oblivious to the fact that this was my sanctuary, my personal narrative laid bare for public consumption without my consent. A deep, cold knot tightened in my chest, pulling my breath shallow. I watched the way the light caught the texture of my paint, a cruel mockery of my ownership. I let out a slow, deliberate exhale, the sound ragged in the quiet room. "This is unacceptable," I stated, the words emerging slow and heavy, each one weighted with suppressed energy. I didn't raise my voice, but the sheer stillness of my posture communicated a profound, simmering displeasure that seemed to vibrate in the air between us.
